text.title: claude-md-creation Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for claude-md-creation. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: A CLAUDE.md must include: Commands (bash blocks for dev/test/lint/build), Structure (directory tree, 2 levels), Conventions (naming, imports, types), and Key Files (table of important entry points). Project Overview is optional but useful.
rule.rationale: Commands is the most frequently referenced section during AI-assisted development — agents need it to run tests, start servers, and apply linters. Without it, agents guess at command names and fail silently.

rule.statement: Keep CLAUDE.md under 150 lines total. A file over 300 lines consumes context budget that could be used for code.
rule.rationale: Claude Code reads CLAUDE.md at session start. Every line of CLAUDE.md is a line not available for the actual code the agent is working on. Concise routing beats comprehensive documentation.

rule.statement: List every command the agent will need in bash code blocks: dev server, test runner, linter, formatter, build, migration, seed. Group by workflow (Development, Testing, Database, Code Quality).
rule.rationale: Agents need exact commands, not descriptions. "Run the tests" is ambiguous; pytest --cov=src -x is actionable. Include flags that agents typically need (coverage, fail-fast, verbose).

rule.statement: Show the directory tree to exactly 2 levels. Add one-line comments after important directories. Do not list every file — only directories and their purpose.
rule.rationale: A 3+ level tree in CLAUDE.md wastes lines on detail the agent can discover by reading. Two levels provides enough context for the agent to know where to look without reading a full file listing.

rule.statement: List concrete naming conventions with examples: file case (snake_case.py), class case (PascalCase), function case (snake_case), constants (UPPER_SNAKE_CASE), import order (stdlib, third-party, local).
rule.rationale: Agents follow naming conventions literally. Vague conventions ("use good names") produce inconsistent output. Specific rules with examples produce consistent output that passes linters without manual fixes.

rule.statement: For Python projects: explicitly name active ruff rule groups. Agents need to know T20 means no print() to avoid triggering pre-commit hooks.
rule.rationale: Ruff rule groups have non-obvious names. An agent that adds print() for debugging will fail the T20 check and require a follow-up fix. Documenting the active rules prevents this class of pre-commit failures.

rule.statement: Root CLAUDE.md in a monorepo must delegate to per-module files using @path/to/AGENTS.md syntax. Never copy module-specific instructions to root.
rule.rationale: Copying module instructions to root creates duplicate maintenance burden and grows the root file past context budget. Each module owns its instructions; root provides routing only.

rule.statement: Review and update CLAUDE.md on every major PR that changes directory layout, build commands, or lint configuration.
rule.rationale: CLAUDE.md is a static snapshot. A 6-month-old file with wrong commands misleads every agent session until it is corrected. Tie updates to the same PR that changes the thing CLAUDE.md describes.

rule.statement: Use <required> notation for environment variables, never example values. Use SECRET_KEY=<required> not SECRET_KEY=abc123.
rule.rationale: Agents may use literal example values found in CLAUDE.md when generating code or configuration. Placeholder notation prevents accidental use of example credentials.
